full_response:
Solution Outline  
1.  Characterise what an optimal solution must look like.  
2.  Design a linear-time algorithm that constructs that solution.  
3.  Prove correctness (exchange argument + induction on the recursion).  
4.  Analyse running time and memory.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Structure of an optimum

Lemma 1 (Greedy structure)  
Let w(1) > w(2) > … > w(n) be the weights in strictly decreasing order.  
For the smallest integer k with 

        w(1)+…+w(k)  ≥  W       and       w(1)+…+w(k-1)  <  W         (∗)

the unique optimum is S⋆ = {w(1), … , w(k)}.

Proof (exchange argument)  
Take any feasible set S of minimum cardinality m.  
Write the m elements of S increasingly: s(1) < s(2) < … < s(m).  
Because all weights are distinct, there exists j such that s(j) ≠ w(j).

Choose the smallest such j.  Then w(j) > s(j).  
Replace s(j) in S by w(j); the total weight of the set does not decrease
whereas the cardinality stays m, so the new set is still feasible and optimal.
Repeating this replacement eventually turns S into {w(1), … , w(m)}.
Condition (∗) forces m = k, proving the lemma. ∎

Hence the task reduces to: “find the smallest k for which the
k heaviest items weigh ≥ W’’  – we never need to consider any lighter item.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  A linear-time algorithm

We avoid an O(n log n) sort with a
selection/partition scheme that is guaranteed O(n).

Pseudocode (deterministic median-of-medians pivot, but a
random pivot gives the same expected O(n)):

```
CHOCOLATE-SELECT(A[1..n], W)
    # returns the set Sˆ of bags to buy
    if W ≤ 0:               # no weight still needed
         return ∅
    if n ≤ 5:               # small base case
         sort A decreasing
         cum ← 0,  S ← ∅
         for x in A:        # at most 5 iterations
              cum ← cum + x
              S   ← S ∪ {x}
              if cum ≥ W: return S

    p ← DETERMINISTIC-MEDIAN-OF-MEDIANS(A)   # Θ(n)
    G ← { x ∈ A | x >  p }       # heavier than the pivot
    L ← { x ∈ A | x <  p }       # lighter than the pivot
    ΣG ← sum of elements of G

    if ΣG ≥ W:                   # the optimum is wholly in G
         return CHOCOLATE-SELECT(G, W)
    else:                        # every optimum must contain all of G
         S ← G
         S ← S ∪ CHOCOLATE-SELECT(L, W - ΣG)
         return S
```

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Proof of correctness

We prove by induction on |A| that the procedure returns S⋆.

Induction hypothesis P(n):  
For every multiset A of n distinct positive weights and every W ≤ ΣA,
`CHOCOLATE-SELECT(A,W)` returns the unique optimum given by Lemma 1.

Base (n ≤ 5).  
The algorithm sorts at most five elements, explicitly takes the heaviest
until W is reached, therefore returns S⋆.

Inductive step.  
Assume P(j) holds for j < n, and run the algorithm on |A| = n.

Let p be the pivot chosen.

Case 1: ΣG ≥ W.  
Lemma 1 says every optimum is contained in G (because replacing any weight in G by a lighter one in L cannot decrease cardinality).  
The algorithm recurses only on G with the same W; |G| < n, so by the
induction hypothesis the recursive call returns the optimum on G,
which is the global optimum.  Hence P(n) holds.

Case 2: ΣG < W.  
Then every optimum must include all of G (if any element of G were left
out, we would be short of at least that weight and would need
an additional bag, contradicting minimality).  The algorithm
adds G to the answer and recurses on L with reduced target W′ = W − ΣG.
Because W′ ≤ ΣL, the recursion is well-defined and |L| < n; by the
induction hypothesis it returns the optimum on L for W′.
The union G ∪ optimum(L,W′) is therefore the global optimum, so P(n)
holds.

By induction, the algorithm is correct for all n. ∎

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Complexity analysis

Each level executes

   – one linear-time deterministic selection for the pivot,  
   – one linear partition that scans the current list once,  
   – constant-time arithmetic on the partial sums.

Whichever branch is taken, the recursion continues with **at most
⌈7n/10⌉** elements (property of median-of-medians) so the sizes of the
lists on the entire recursion path are disjoint.  
Thus the total number of element examinations is bounded by c·n
for a constant c, i.e. T(n) = Θ(n).

Space: the partition can be done in-place, and the recursion depth is
O(1) with median-of-medians (maximum depth ≤ O(log n)), so the extra
space is O(log n) or O(1) with tail-recursion elimination.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Final answer

\boxed{%
An optimum consists of the k largest weights, where k is the smallest
index whose cumulative weight reaches W.
In O(n) time we find it by repeatedly (i) selecting a pivot in linear
time, (ii) partitioning into heavier G and lighter L, (iii) if
ΣG ≥ W recurse on G, else output G and recurse on L with the remaining
required weight.
The total work over all recursions is Θ(n) and the algorithm returns
exactly that optimum set by the exchange argument above.}

Confidence: 79%
